text: I knew Como would be beautiful, but I was surprised by just how stunning it was. With how popular it is, I thought I might be a bit disappointed or find it overrated. I was wrong. We stayed in the hills of Bellano, which felt like a perfect place to avoid the masses. There were tourists of course (like ourselves), but we didn't feel overwhelmed at all. Renting a boat for an afternoon was the absolute highlight and for me a must-do. We were able to drive ourselves around the lake to see the stunning scenery and famous villas and villages, and of course, stop for a swim. The people were also incredibly lovely. A taxi driver even gave us a bottle of prosecco because he couldn't pick us up one night.
label: experience

text: I was recently checking out some hotels and there were hotels asking to check in at 3pm, some even at 5pm and checkout as early as 8am. And the prices are also over the roof. I remember back in the day where checkin was 11am and checkout used to be at 10am.
label: advice

text: I'm an American, and I'm currently in Barcelona on a short study abroad program (about 3 weeks). I arrived on Friday. For context, traveling abroad has always been a dream of mine. I've always wanted to get out of my small town to witness different cultures. Now i'm here, and i'm a little underwhelmed. I definitely witnessed a big culture shock. I speak very basic Spanish, but not a whole lot to have a conversation. People from Barcelona dress very nice. I dress like a normal American (Basketball shorts and a T-Shirt). I usually get weird looks from people because of this. The reason why this is disappointing is that I had really high expectations. I have been told by everybody that the locals from Barcelona are super nice, and that the city is very fun. If i'm being honest, I just feel very lonely. I want to enjoy this trip and I want to have fun. Does anybody have any advice for first-time solo travelers, getting over lonliness, or truly enjoying your trip? Remember to be patient with me. This is my first time.
label: experience

text: I've bought the cheapest flight every two days for two weeks straight. On May 10th I decided that for the next two weeks I'll go on holiday by buying the cheapest flight the next day. I left from Romania to Malta on May 11 with a ticket worth 15.50 Euro. It was my first time in a hostel in Malta, such a cool experience. My goal was to stay two days in each country, my searching criteria was just buying plane tickets to places I've never seen before. I ended up seeing Malta, Bari (Italy), Wien (Austria), Warsaw (Poland), London (UK), Malmo (Sweden), Copenhagen (Denmark) and Budapest (Hungary). Met quite a few interesting individuals, seen bunch of things for the first time, had some time off work and other day-to-day duties and overall enjoyed the experience quite a lot.
label: experience

text: Minimum pain, Maximum gain that is my motto nowadays. There are stunning hikes all over the world. My heart keeps on telling me to go there. My head and knees scream: Don't even think of going, crazy old fool. So nowadays I select my hikes carefully. If you are a fit person running a marathon then do not need to read anymore. But if you are unfit, stressed, well past sell-by-date couch potato, then come and I'll show you how to cheat and still enjoy nature. Dolomites are hikers' paradise. I decided on Seceda hike. Trust me, if you want to cheat by taking the cable car and can manage about 10 km, pick Seceda over any hike in the whole Alps.
label: advice

text: I had such a pleasant experience visiting Pittsburgh for the first time. I love going to different cities across the US, especially the ones that can get overlooked. It's like a city that just popped out of the mountains and almost shouldn't even be there (in a good way), but it's beautiful. Pittsburgh has so much character. I think one of my favorite things were the steel bridges, they have such character to them and really make the scenery around the river. I went there with PNC Park (Pirates baseball) being the number 1 goal of the trip. It didn't disappoint at all! Just an incredibly terrific ballpark with amazing views.
label: experience

text: Spent a few days in Switzerland earlier this year, and honestly, I get the hype now. The trip itself was half the fun. Driving through the mountains, taking the car train through the Alps, stopping for food, sitting in the sun with a Swiss wheat beer and snow everywhere around you. Hard to complain. Zermatt was probably my favorite part. The Matterhorn at sunset looked almost fake, especially with the village lights coming on below it. Zurich was a nice ending too, much calmer than I expected, especially around the river in the evening.
label: experience

text: My wife and I were awestruck by Iceland. It is, to date, my favorite destination. The silence and calm of driving the farmland countryside and the overwhelming beauty of the landscapes drew us in. I'm not sure I'll ever love a destination the way I did Iceland. If I could return to one place we visited, it would be the Eastern region. The landscapes and land masses out there were otherworldly. We also saw the northern lights there for the first time in our lives which shook us.
label: experience

text: I'm not even mad it's expensive. This place is like a living postcard on every single step. I made more than 200 photos in 3 days. These are just some of them.

text: Slovenia was truly an amazing experience with friendly people and beautiful nature. Incredible to be able to drive from the Alps to the coast within a few short hours. Highly recommend making a trip here.

text: Quite late with this post, but thought I'd share my experience. Made an unpopular choice and went to Plitvice lakes in Croatia in the winter. Still beautiful and gorgeous, but much cheaper and much less crowded than in the summer. We were hoping for snow, but the atmosphere was still magical. The first day (we were there for 3), the mist made the entire place just surreal. So yeah, for someone wondering if they should go - absolutely! But I'd recommend going in shoulder season, or off season like we did.
label: experience

In Krabi I did Phi Phi island and hong Island group tours. In Krabi I was staying near Ao Nang beach.

text: Spent about a week in France this June with my wife and our young daughter, splitting time between Paris and Provence. Blue hour on the Seine is the move for the Eiffel Tower. We caught it from a boat right as the lights came on, and it beats every daytime angle we tried. The Louvre is overwhelming but worth it. Pro tip we wish we'd known sooner: the glass pyramid entrance line is brutal, so book a timed slot in advance. Montmartre rewards wandering. Sacré-Cœur at dusk with everyone sitting on the steps is a vibe, but the side streets and staircases coming down the hill were our favorite part.
label: advice

text: Hey guys, I wanted to share my itinerary for this trip I did last april. Start: Arrival at night in Antalya, rented a car and drove down to çiralı. Day 1: Olympos ancient city in the morning. Chimera: naturally occurring fires that have been constantly burning for 2000+ years. Day 2: Lycian way hike! Demre to Kaş. Day 3: Second day of hiking with a long stop at Simena and Kekova. Day 4: Hike in the morning, hitchhiked back to Demre to get the car and drove to Kaş. Day 5: Daytrip to Kastellorizo, a small greek island 15 min away from the turkish coast by speedboat. Day 6-7: Resting above Kabak, and chilling at the beach. Day 8: Drive back to Antalya.
label: planning

text: Hello there travelers! We spent 9 days in Morocco last month and I want to share our experience with you. We rented a Seat Arona from Sixt at the airport for 500 EURO including full insurance, extra driver and unlimited mileage. The car was reasonable, worked good enough for the roads that we went through and the renting experience was smooth. We drove nearly 2.000 kms in the country.

text: I've always thought that the Leaning Tower of Pisa and the Field of Miracles was more of an overblown tourist trap more than anything, but honestly, the place is so incredibly beautiful that it makes up for the crowds and the blistering summer heat. Lucca is unbelievable. A small walled city filled with immaculate architecture and several extremely ornate Romanesque churches. The red tiled roofs alone make the town incredibly charming, and flea markets adorned every square.

text: I've been on a 4-day trip to København (Copenhagen) and nearby places such as Malmö. Copenhagen is truly a magnificent city, featuring many stunning places and experiences. text: Spend 7 days in Northern Pakistan mostly in the Gilgit Baltistan region and it's one of the best experience of traveling so far. text: Štrbské Pleso, Slovakia. Some places don't need filters. This is one of them. Tucked high in the High Tatras, Štrbské Pleso feels like someone accidentally dropped an alpine lake into a postcard. Crystal clear water, mountain peaks reflected like glass, forests that smell impossibly fresh. If you're road-tripping through Central Europe, don't sleep on Slovakia. Everyone talks about the Alps, but the High Tatras quietly deliver the same feeling with far fewer crowds.
label: experience

text: Wanted to share this because it could save someone a lot of money and stress. I rented a Toyota Camry from SIXT at Albuquerque International Airport for a 3-day trip. About a week after I returned the car, I got a damage report and then an invoice for $3,015.52. I disputed it in writing, attached my timestamped photos, and laid out specific questions. A few days after sending it, Sixt called and closed the case immediately once they saw the pickup photos. Before you drive off in any rental, from any company: Take photos AND a video walkaround, narrating out loud what you see.
label: advice

text: 1-6 Algiers. 7-9 Timimoun. 10-14 Desert around Timimoun. At the end of January, I spent a week in Algeria. Almost all nationalities require a visa to enter the country; I applied for mine in person at the embassy in Berlin. One unique aspect of Algeria is currency exchange. There is an official rate of approximately 1€ = 150 dinars. However, the unofficial rate when exchanging money with private individuals is around 1€ = 275 dinars. So you get almost double the value for your euros. The trip to Timimoun was well worth it. It's an oasis town with unique architecture.
label: experience

text: Outside of Tbilisi you may encounter a petty, pathetic little scam where the railway ticket office can't sell you a ticket and tells you you have to pay double onboard. The dissipated looking train conductors then try to add 4 GEL or more to the doubled price. These are the kind of people who steal toilet paper from public restrooms.

text: I went with my partner on a 10 day tour of Ireland. We booked with Brendan Tours, which he had been on before. It was a group of 49 people. I had never been on a group tour before. I was not ready for changing hotel rooms every day, getting up so early, and having very little time at each site. We even had to take food to go and eat on the bus. While the tour guide and bus driver were very good, the itinerary was crazy.

text: I'm posting this as a reminder to NEVER take the first response from an airline as the final answer. They are banking on you giving up. On June 12, my connecting flight from San José to Madrid got massively delayed, causing me to miss my connection home to Belgium. As soon as I filed my claim, I got the classic corporate brush-off email from Iberia. Instead of accepting their lie, I immediately replied and laid down the law. The Result: I just opened my inbox to a total capitulation from a different customer relations agent paying 600 euros.

text:
Hello everyone, A friend and I are going to be doing a road trip through Iceland in early September and was hoping to get some advice/suggestions. We have done our research (websites, TripAdvisor, Lonely Planet, etc..) but we thought the Reddit community would be able to offer some expert advice. Here is the plan:

Friday - Fly to Iceland

Saturday - Reykjavik

Sunday - Reykjavik

Monday - Golden Circle

Tuesday - Reykjavik to Vik (Seljalandsfoss Waterfall, Wrecked Airplane, Seljavallalaug, Black Sand Beach)

Wednesday - Vik to Hofn (Black Waterfall, Svinafellsjokull, Fjallsarion Glacier Lagoon, Jokullsarlon) Alternative would be Landmannalaugar

Thursday - Hofn to Reykjahlio (not much to see?)

Friday - Reykjahlio to Stykkisholmur (Myvatn, Godafoss Watrefall) Alternative would be going through Kjalvegur

Saturday - Stykkisholmur to Reykjavik (Kirkjufell and Snaefellsjokull)

Sunday - Grindavik and flight home

For those who live in Iceland/have visited Iceland, what do you think of this road trip itinerary? I did have a few questions I was also hoping to get some input on.

Is Landmannalaugar, Hornstrandir Nature Reserve, and Kjalvegur worth visiting? Landmannalaugar and Kjalvegur seem a bit out of the way and seem to require a 4x4 vehicle. I am looking to add an extra day to the trip, so I could add this in if it's worth it.

Do you really need a 4x4 vehicle to traverse around Iceland? I would like to see the abandoned DC-3 on Solheimasandur beach, but we would mainly be sticking on the main road (unless I am mistaken and some of the locations listed above require 4x4).

Do the towns I play on staying at overnight seem to be reasonable places? (Tried to find locations that wouldn't require us to drive 10+ hours each day BUT didn't do much research into actual hotels and hostels).

Any recommendations on car rentals? From work, we get a free upgrade with National but they do require a international drivers license (we are from the US)

Overall, seems to be a decent trip? Going in September seems like a good idea because cost of traveling seems to be lower. Appreciate any advice and input!
label: planning

text:
Hi! My fiancé and I are going to Jackson for the first time this July. I put together an itinerary that’s definitely a little ambitious (I’m aware lol), but I’d rather have more options and cut things in the moment than be scrambling to figure out what to do while we’re there.

Would really appreciate any feedback, suggestions, must-dos, or things that aren’t worth it. We’re not familiar with the area and want to make the most of the trip!

1 (Monday – arrival)

Day

6:00am – 11:15am: Flight

Pick up rental car

1:00pm: Lunch @ Dornan’s Pizza

3:00pm: Moose Entrance → Snake River Overlook → Oxbow Bend

6:40pm: Stop for bear spray / groceries, grab takeout dinner (Koi Sushi & Poke)

8:00pm: Check into Airbnb in Jackson

Day 2 (Tuesday – Jenny Lake + Leigh Lake)

6:00–6:30am: Cowboy coffee & breakfast in car

8:00am – 1:00pm: Hidden Falls, Inspiration Point, Jenny Lake Loop (~3.5 miles, ~2–3 hrs depending on route) (·Jenny lake boating to hidden falls, Hike to hidden falls (0.5 miles), Hike to Inspiration Point (0.5 miles), (3 miles) down cascade canyon and back, Jenny lake horse trail back to visitor center (2.5 mile back)

Lunch on trail

2:00pm – 3:00pm: Leigh Lake (~3 miles out and back, ~1 hour, optional swim)

6:00pm: Dinner @ Glorietta's

Moo’s Ice Cream after

Day 3 (Wednesday – Phelps Lake + Jackson)

5:15am: Sunrise @ Mormon Row

7:30am – 11:30am: Phelps Lake (~7 miles, moderate, ~2.5–3 hrs, jumping rock)

12:00pm: Mama Mimi @ Rendezvous Park

1:30pm: Lunch @ Pinky G’s Pizza

3:00 – 5:00pm: Rest / shower

6:00 – 7:00pm: Cowboy Coaster

7:15 – 8:15pm: Dinner @ Aurora (Snow King)

Day 4 (Thursday – views + rafting + Taggart?)

5:15am: Optional sunrise @ Schwabacher Landing

7:00 – 7:30am: Lunch Tree Hill (~0.5 miles, ~30 min)

8:30am: Signal Mountain Summit Road (10 min drive to top)

10:30am: Coffee + bagels @ Snake River Roasting Co.

11:15am – 4:00pm: Whitewater rafting

4:30pm: Rest / shower

6:30pm: Dinner @ Gun Barrel Steakhouse

Moo’s Ice Cream or Jackson Drugs after

Day 5 (Friday – Jackson activities)

11:00am: Brunch @ Persephone

1:00pm: Cloud Veil Rooftop or Blue Heron Lounge

3:00pm: Snake River Brewery

5:00pm: Dinner @ The Local Restaurant & Bar

8:00pm: Rodeo (arrive ~7:15)

10:00pm: Million Dollar Cowboy Bar

Day 6 (Saturday – shopping + chill)

10:00am: Coffee (Snake River Roasting or Cowboy Coffee)

1:30pm: Million Dollar Cowboy Bar

Afternoon: more exploring / shopping

5:15pm: Head back to pack

7:00pm: Dinner @ Roosevelt’s

Day 7 (Sunday)

Travel day

text:
Hi everyone!

I’m planning a massive 7-week solo trip across Brazil in May/June 2026. I’ve previously done long-term backpacking in South East Asia and solo trips in Colombia, Guatemala, and Mexico, so I’m used to "latino" logistics and long bus rides.

However, Brazil is a different beast and I want to know if my itinerary is feasible or if I’m headed for a burnout.

The Itinerary:

May 6 - 10 : Tabatinga (Boat from Iquitos) -> Flight to Manaus (3 nights).

May 10 - 14 : Boat from Manaus to Santarém/Alter do Chão (30h boat ride).

May 14 - 18 : Flight to Rio de Janeiro (4 nights). Main goal: Catching Fluminense vs São Paulo on 17/05 with a friend

May 18 - 24 : Flight to São Luís -> Lençóis Maranhenses. Plan: 2/3-night trek (Atins -> Santo Amaro).

May 24 - 28 : Flight to Salvador (4 nights)

May 28 - June 3 : Car rental from Salvador to Chapada Diamantina (6 nights). Plan: 3-day Vale do Pati trek.

June 3 - 6 : Drive to Itacaré (3 nights) then back to Salvador to drop the car.

June 6 - 11 : Flight to Belo Horizonte -> Bus to Ouro Preto (2 nights).

June 11 - 23 : Bus back to Rio -> Ilha Grande (4 nights) -> Paraty (3 nights) -> Finish in Rio (3 nights).

My Main Doubts:

The Salvador-Chapada-Itacaré Loop: Is renting a car in Salvador and driving to Itacaré/Chapada alone a good idea or should I rely on buses?

The "North to Salvador" jump: I’m skipping Jericoacoara/Fortaleza to save time. Am I missing out on too much since I’m already doing Lençóis?

Fatigue: Does this look too rushed? I feel like I have "recovery" spots (Alter, Itacaré, Ilha Grande), but the travel days are long.

Am I delusional? Any advice on specific legs of the trip or logistics would be amazing :)

Thank you in advance <3
label: planning
